questions.scenario: An alert says failed checkout requests are above threshold. A teammate wants to change the SQL firewall immediately because the latest exception mentions SQL. What should the on-call person check first?

options.text: Confirm the failed request rate, inspect recent request traces, and verify whether the SQL dependency is failing for the same operation IDs.
Open the firewall first, because observability is only useful after the app is fixed.
Disable the alert, because repeated notifications slow down the incident response.
Restart the Container App before checking evidence, because restarts are faster than querying logs.

questions.explanation: The first response should connect metric scope, request evidence, and dependency evidence. A firewall change may be needed, but it should follow evidence.

questions.scenario: A dashboard shows high latency but no increase in failed requests. Users report slow checkout, not broken checkout. Which next evidence is most useful?

options.text: Dependency timing for checkout operations, because latency can come from a downstream call even when requests still succeed.
Action group email addresses, because notification routing explains request duration.
The storage account SKU, because every latency incident starts with object storage.
The resource group tag list, because tags show which code path is slow.

questions.explanation: Latency without failures often points to a slow dependency or internal path. Dependency timing is the next useful evidence for that request story.
